findings.title: Batch timeout in matching endpoint does not cancel underlying scorer work
findings.description: match_jobs.ts implements timeout handling at lines 598-710 using Promise.race() with a timeout promise. When timeout fires, the race rejects and code breaks to the next batch. However, the underlying matchingService.matchJobs() call (line 643-652) is not cancellable via AbortSignal, so it continues executing in the background. This creates overlapping work, wasted CPU, and inconsistent partial-failure accounting during high load.

findings.title: All npm dependencies unpinned (caret ^) — non-deterministic builds despite lockfile
findings.description: Every entry in package.json dependencies and devDependencies uses the caret (^) range specifier, e.g. "react": "^18.2.0". While package-lock.json is committed (providing determinism for npm ci), the actual semver ranges mean any npm install without --ci can silently upgrade to a new minor/patch that introduces a breaking change. Netlify uses npm ci so production is safe, but local developer installs may diverge.

proof_hooks.summary: All dependencies use caret ranges

repro_steps: Delete node_modules and package-lock.json
npm install
Dependency tree may differ from CI
findings.impact: Low risk day-to-day. Risk increases when a dependency releases a breaking minor (unusual but it happens). No immediate action required.

suggested_fix.approach: Keep caret ranges as-is (they are idiomatic). Ensure package-lock.json is always committed and CI always uses npm ci. Optionally add Dependabot to get automated PRs when dependencies need updating.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: Adding Dependabot is additive and reduces long-term supply chain risk.

history.notes: Identified during deployment safety review. Low priority — npm ci and committed lockfile mitigate the risk.

findings.title: daily_summaries: full table scan on analytics_events — SELECT * with no LIMIT loads full day into memory
findings.description: netlify/functions/daily_summaries.ts fetches every row from analytics_events for a 24-hour window using .select('*') with no row limit. All matching rows are pulled into the Netlify function's Node.js heap, then reduced with in-process JavaScript. On a busy day the analytics_events table could have tens of thousands of rows — each containing a full JSONB properties column. This causes: (1) high DB transfer cost, (2) elevated memory in the function, (3) slow cold starts for a scheduled function that runs every 24 hours. The aggregation (DAU, event counts per feature) is trivial SQL that the database can compute orders of magnitude faster. Additionally, there is no idempotency guard, so re-invocations on the same day insert duplicate rows into analytics_daily_summaries (see related finding f-b1c8c1a4).

proof_hooks.summary: daily_summaries.ts uses RPC for server-side aggregation

findings.impact: Resolved. Function memory and DB transfer cost are now proportional to the number of distinct metric keys, not the number of events.

suggested_fix.approach: Already fixed.

history.notes: Discovered via static analysis of daily_summaries.ts. select('*') with no limit on a time-series events table is a textbook full-table-scan anti-pattern.
Verified fixed: daily_summaries.ts now uses supabase.rpc('aggregate_daily_analytics') instead of SELECT * from analytics_events. Upsert uses onConflict (see f-b1c8c1a4 also fixed).

findings.title: build_auto_apply_queue: N+1 — user_personas queried individually per rule inside a serial for loop
findings.description: netlify/functions/build_auto_apply_queue.ts processes auto-apply rules in a for...of loop (line 169). Inside each iteration, if rule.persona_id is set, it issues an individual SELECT to user_personas for that persona (lines 175-186). If a user has N rules with distinct persona_ids, this fires N sequential DB round-trips. findings.title: job_alerts: N+1 profile aggregation — aggregateUserProfile fires 4 DB queries per user in a serial loop
findings.description: netlify/functions/job_alerts.ts iterates over all users who have job alerts enabled (line 55: for (const user of users)) and calls aggregateUserProfile(supabase, user.id) for each one (line 58). aggregateUserProfile (src/lib/scoring/profileAggregator.ts line 318) runs 4 parallel queries per user: user_professional_profiles, job_preferences, resumes, and persona_preferences. findings.description: netlify/functions/ingest_jobs.ts calls resolveCompanyId(j.company) inside Promise.all(jobsToInsert.map(async (j) => ...)) at lines 2112-2119, and again at lines 2459, 2582, and 2708 for other ingestion paths. netlify/functions/utils/companyResolver.ts maintains a module-level Map cache that deduplicates repeated company names within a single Lambda invocation. However, for a batch of jobs with N distinct company names not yet in cache, this fires N individual supabase.rpc('resolve_company_id', ...) calls in parallel. Each call is an RPC round-trip. A batch approach — loading all company IDs for a set of names in one query — would reduce N round-trips to 1.

proof_hooks.summary: bulkResolveCompanyIds added to companyResolver.ts
bulkResolveCompanyIds called before Greenhouse batch loop

findings.impact: For a batch of 100 jobs with 50 distinct company names: previously 50 parallel RPC calls, now 1 bulk DB query + 50 cache hits.

suggested_fix.approach: Already fixed in this run.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: bulkResolveCompanyIds queries the companies table by name (case-insensitive). The existing resolveCompanyId uses a fuzzy-match RPC. Names not found in the bulk query fall through to null in cache; resolveCompanyId will still fire the RPC as fallback for cache misses. Verify names not matched by direct equality are still resolved correctly via the fallback.
tests_needed: Unit test: bulkResolveCompanyIds for 10 distinct names fires exactly 1 DB query
Unit test: subsequent resolveCompanyId calls return cached values without DB round-trips

history.notes: Module-level cache is good for repeated runs but not for first-encounter batch scenarios. Bulk resolution is the natural next step.

findings.title: useMatchedJobs: custom useState+useEffect pattern bypasses React Query caching, re-fetches on every mount
findings.description: src/hooks/useMatchedJobs.ts manages server state with local useState and useEffect (lines 85-217) rather than using the React Query instance (useQuery) that is already in scope in the rest of the app (e.g., useJobs.ts uses @tanstack/react-query). Every time a component mounting useMatchedJobs re-renders or navigates away and back, fetchMatches() fires a full HTTP request to /.netlify/functions/get_matched_jobs. findings.title: daily_summaries has no idempotency guard — re-invocations on the same day insert duplicate summary rows
findings.description: netlify/functions/daily_summaries.ts builds analytics summary rows and upserts them into analytics_daily_summaries. The code currently uses .insert() (or .upsert() — exact conflict behavior depends on the table schema which was not accessible). If the scheduled function runs twice on the same day (e.g., Netlify retry after timeout, manual trigger, or schedule drift), duplicate rows for the same (day, metric_key) will be inserted. This corrupts dashboard aggregations that assume one row per day per metric. Additionally, this is a question rather than a definitive bug because the actual conflict behavior depends on the analytics_daily_summaries schema (whether there is a UNIQUE constraint on (day, metric_key)). This must be verified.

proof_hooks.summary: Upsert with onConflict ensures idempotency

findings.impact: Resolved. Multiple invocations on the same day are safe.

suggested_fix.approach: Already fixed.

history.notes: Emitted as 'question' type because the actual conflict behavior cannot be confirmed without inspecting the analytics_daily_summaries schema. Needs a quick DB check before categorizing as a confirmed bug.
Verified fixed: daily_summaries.ts uses .upsert(summaries, { onConflict: 'day,metric_key' }) making the function fully idempotent.

findings.description: LoadingState.tsx (the primary loading component used in DashboardPage, JobsPage, App.tsx, AuthGuard, TargetManagement, and more) renders a spinner and optional text message with no ARIA live region. Screen readers will not announce when loading starts or completes. The same gap exists in Spinner.tsx (legacy secondary spinner). Contrast: Toast.tsx in the same codebase correctly uses role='status' and aria-live='polite' as the model to follow.

proof_hooks.summary: LoadingState renders spinner and message with no ARIA live region or role
Spinner.tsx also has no ARIA attributes

findings.impact: Screen reader users have no way to know the page is loading or when it has finished. After loading completes, if the newly rendered content is not in focus, the user receives no notification. Fails WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.3 (Status Messages).

suggested_fix.approach: Add role='status' and aria-live='polite' to the outermost container in LoadingState.tsx. Use the message prop value (defaulting to 'Loading') as an aria-label on the container or include it as visually-hidden text. Apply the same attributes to Spinner.tsx.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: Additive ARIA attributes only. No visual change.

history.notes: Fixed in PR: audit-user-flow-copy-consistency

findings.title: AddApplicationModal and QuickLogApplicationModal use raw overlay divs — missing role='dialog', aria-modal, and focus trap
findings.description: AddApplicationModal.tsx and QuickLogApplicationModal.tsx implement their own modal overlay using raw Tailwind div elements without role='dialog', aria-modal='true', aria-labelledby, or a focus trap. The design system Modal.tsx (used by other modals in the app) has all of these — role='dialog', aria-modal='true', aria-labelledby, Escape key support, and focus restoration on close. The two application modals bypass Modal.tsx entirely, creating a two-tier accessibility quality: feature modals are inaccessible, generic modals are not.

proof_hooks.summary: AddApplicationModal dialog div now has role/aria attributes
QuickApplyModal dialog div now has role/aria attributes

findings.impact: Screen reader users: the modal is not announced as a dialog, focus is not trapped (users can tab into background content), and the modal heading is not announced. Fails WCAG 2.1 SC 4.1.2 (Name, Role, Value). The add-application flow is one of the most frequently used flows in the app.

suggested_fix.approach: Refactor AddApplicationModal and QuickLogApplicationModal to use Modal.tsx as the outer shell, or manually add role='dialog', aria-modal='true', aria-labelledby linked to the modal title, and the focus trap implementation from Modal.tsx.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: Modal.tsx may enforce its own layout/styling. A visual regression check is needed after migration. Focus trap must be verified to work correctly on mobile browsers.

findings.title: No section-level error boundaries — a single component error anywhere crashes the entire page to the root recovery UI
findings.description: The codebase has exactly two ErrorBoundary instances: one in main.tsx (outermost) and one in App.tsx wrapping AppInner. There are no granular boundaries around individual feature sections: job feed, resume builder, dashboard widgets, application list, or target management panel. A JavaScript render error in any of these sections will bubble all the way to the root ErrorBoundary, showing the generic 'Something went wrong — Try again / Go to Dashboard' UI and losing all user state across the entire app. The ErrorBoundary component is well-built and supports a custom fallback prop — it simply is not deployed at the section level.

proof_hooks.summary: Only root-level ErrorBoundary in main.tsx
ErrorBoundary supports fallback prop but unused at section level

findings.impact: A rendering error in the job feed crashes the application, forcing a full reload and loss of any unsaved application form state or resume draft. Per LYRA agent instructions, missing error boundaries around major sections are classified as Major Enhancement.

suggested_fix.approach: Wrap each major page section in a scoped ErrorBoundary with a tailored fallback prop. Priority sections: JobsPage job feed, DashboardPage widget area, ResumeBuilder editor, ApplicationsPage list, TargetsPage list. Example fallback: 'Jobs couldn\'t load — try refreshing this section' with a retry button.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: Boundaries must not be too granular (per-row or per-card) to avoid masking systemic failures. Section-level granularity is appropriate.

history.notes: Section-level ErrorBoundary wrappers added to JobsPage, DashboardPage, ApplicationsPage, TargetsPage, DocumentsPage with tailored fallback UIs.

findings.title: Product voice is undefined — copy.ts, errorTranslation.ts, and inline JSX use three distinct tones with no guiding voice document
findings.description: The app's copy uses at least three distinct tones without a defining voice guide: (1) warm/empathetic/branded in copy.ts ('Something went sideways', 'We lost our bearings', 'Your story starts here', 'Nothing saved yet — when you find a role worth holding onto, save it here. We\'ll keep it safe.'); (2) clinical/formal in errorTranslation.ts ('Something went wrong.', 'You don\'t have permission for this action or your session expired.'); (3) blunt/informal inline in DashboardPage JSX ('That\'s rough', 'You\'re here. That\'s the first step.'). No voice guide document exists. Without one, contributors write in their own style and inconsistencies accumulate with every new feature.

proof_hooks.summary: Warm/branded tone in copy.ts empty states section
Clinical/formal tone in errorTranslation.ts

findings.impact: Without a defined voice, copy inconsistencies grow with every feature addition. Erodes brand coherence at scale and makes onboarding contributors harder.

suggested_fix.approach: Define a short voice guide (3-5 principles with do/don\'t examples). Proposed default principles: (1) Human and direct — no corporate speak; (2) Warm but not precious — empathetic without being saccharine; (3) Sentence case for all UI labels and headings; (4) Error messages name the next action, not just the problem; (5) Never expose internal system terms to users. Embed as a comment block at the top of copy.ts and/or as docs/VOICE.md.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: No code change required — this is a documentation/alignment artifact.

history.notes: Emitted per LYRA stop condition: product voice undefined

findings.title: aggregate_daily_analytics SECURITY DEFINER function missing REVOKE FROM PUBLIC — callable by any authenticated user
findings.description: Migration 20260305223000_add_aggregate_daily_analytics_rpc.sql creates aggregate_daily_analytics as SECURITY DEFINER with only GRANT EXECUTE TO service_role but no REVOKE ALL FROM PUBLIC. Without an explicit REVOKE, any authenticated (or anon) caller can invoke this function and read aggregate analytics data, bypassing the intended service_role-only access control. suggested_fix.approach: Add REVOKE ALL ON FUNCTION public.aggregate_daily_analytics(date) FROM PUBLIC before the GRANT EXECUTE TO service_role line in migration 20260305223000. Fixed in this audit run.

findings.title: Promise.all with resolveCompanyId creates unnecessary async overhead after bulkResolveCompanyIds pre-warms cache
findings.description: After bulkResolveCompanyIds() populates the in-memory companyCache, ingest_jobs.ts uses Promise.all(jobsToInsert.map(async (j) => { const companyId = await resolveCompanyId(j.company) })) at lines 2114-2115, 2462-2463, 2587-2588, and 2715-2716. Each resolveCompanyId() call checks the cache and returns instantly. However, Promise.all() creates async wrappers and awaits even though the cache hits are synchronous. This adds unnecessary event-loop delays and memory pressure for large batches (100+ jobs).

proof_hooks.summary: bulkResolveCompanyIds populates companyCache
Promise.all with resolveCompanyId at all 4 ingestion paths

findings.impact: For a batch of 100 jobs with 50 pre-warmed cache entries: overhead of 50 Promise creations and await statements across 4 ingestion code paths.

suggested_fix.approach: Export a getCachedCompanyId(name) helper from companyResolver.ts that reads the cache directly without async wrapper. Call bulkResolveCompanyIds(), then map jobs synchronously using getCachedCompanyId(). Fallback to resolveCompanyId() only for cache misses.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: companyCache is module-scoped with no export. Adding a getter requires careful testing of the fallback path.

history.notes: Re-audit of companyResolver/ingest_jobs. bulkResolveCompanyIds pre-warms cache, but Promise.all still creates unnecessary async overhead for cache hits.

findings.title: companyCache module scope: is direct access intentionally hidden?
findings.description: companyResolver.ts defines companyCache as a module-level const Map but does not export it. ingest_jobs.ts must call resolveCompanyId() (async) even for cache hits after bulkResolveCompanyIds() pre-warms the cache. Is the lack of export intentional to force cache access through the async wrapper, or an oversight?

proof_hooks.summary: companyCache is const, not exported

findings.impact: Architectural clarity. If sync cache reads are acceptable, document it. If async-only is intended, add a comment explaining why.

suggested_fix.approach: Review design intent. If sync cache reads are acceptable, export a getCachedCompanyId(name) function. If async-only is intended, add a comment explaining why.

history.notes: Raised as clarification question during re-audit of companyResolver/ingest_jobs performance path.

findings.title: bulkResolveCompanyIds uses case-sensitive IN clause and omits canonical_name; RPC fallback is case-insensitive and checks both columns
findings.description: bulkResolveCompanyIds() queries .select('id, name').in('name', originalNames) — case-sensitive equality. The resolve_company_id RPC uses LOWER(name) = LOWER(p_name) OR LOWER(canonical_name) = LOWER(p_name) — case-insensitive and also checks canonical_name. Companies with case-mismatched names or known only by canonical_name are NOT found by the bulk query, still triggering N individual RPC fallback calls per batch.

proof_hooks.summary: bulkResolveCompanyIds uses case-sensitive .in() on name column
resolve_company_id RPC uses LOWER() and checks canonical_name
proof_hooks.query: SELECT id INTO v_id FROM companies WHERE LOWER(name) = LOWER(p_name) OR LOWER(canonical_name) = LOWER(p_name) LIMIT 1

findings.impact: For feeds with inconsistent company name casing, bulk query miss rate is high. Fix f-6e5c06b7 only partially reduces DB round-trips.

suggested_fix.approach: Create a bulk RPC that accepts an array of names and returns matches using the same case-insensitive + canonical_name logic as resolve_company_id.

history.notes: Net-new finding from targeted re-audit. Case sensitivity mismatch between bulk query and RPC limits bulk-query hit rate.

findings.title: companyCache never expires; stale company_id values persist across warm Lambda invocations
findings.description: companyResolver.ts defines a module-level Map companyCache with no expiration. If a company is renamed, merged, or deleted from the companies table after the cache was populated, subsequent ingest runs on the same warm Lambda container return stale company_id values. clearCompanyCache() is exported (lines 110-112) but never called from ingest_jobs.ts.

proof_hooks.summary: Module-level cache with no expiration
clearCompanyCache() exported but never called from ingest_jobs.ts

findings.impact: Low likelihood. If it occurs: new job records get incorrect or dangling company_id FKs, corrupting company-based filtering.

suggested_fix.approach: Call clearCompanyCache() at the top of the handler in ingest_jobs.ts before runIngestion(). Clears cache once per Lambda invocation.

history.notes: Net-new finding. clearCompanyCache() is exported but never called. Identified during logic re-audit of companyResolver.ts.

findings.title: ingest_jobs handler accepts any HTTP method; no method guard before runIngestion
findings.description: The handler in ingest_jobs.ts (line 3547) does not check event.httpMethod before calling runIngestion(). Any HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.) triggers a full ingestion run. Other Netlify Functions in this codebase check for POST-only and return 405 for other methods.

proof_hooks.summary: Handler entry point — no httpMethod check

findings.impact: Any HTTP method triggers ingestion. Amplifies the auth-bypass risk in f-a053d9a2.

suggested_fix.approach: Add if (event.httpMethod !== 'POST') return { statusCode: 405, body: JSON.stringify({ error: 'Method Not Allowed' }) } at the top of the handler.

history.notes: Net-new finding. Identified during logic re-audit of ingest_jobs.ts handler.

findings.title: ingest_jobs handler has no authentication gate; any caller can trigger source-level ingestion
findings.description: The handler in ingest_jobs.ts (line 3547) accepts any incoming HTTP request and calls runIngestion(requestedSlug) without validating any credential, API key, JWT, or CSRF token. An unauthenticated attacker can trigger single-source ingestion (e.g., GET /.netlify/functions/ingest_jobs?source=adzuna_us), consuming paid API quotas for Adzuna, Jooble, CareerJet, WhatJobs, Reed UK, and other sources. Violates expectations section 2.1.

proof_hooks.summary: Handler entry point — no auth check before runIngestion
Unauthenticated GET triggers Adzuna ingestion run
steps: curl https://<site>/.netlify/functions/ingest_jobs?source=adzuna_us
No Authorization header required.
Observe: HTTP 200 with full ingestion results, Adzuna API quota consumed.

proof_hooks.summary: Paid API keys consumed by unauthenticated trigger
proof_hooks.key: ADZUNA_APP_ID, ADZUNA_APP_KEY, JOOBLE_API_KEY, CAREERJET_API_KEY, WHATJOBS_API_KEY, REED_API_KEY

findings.impact: Attacker can exhaust daily API quotas for paid job sources and trigger repeated DB writes using the admin Supabase client.

suggested_fix.approach: Add a pre-shared secret check: require an X-Ingestion-Key header matching a INGEST_SECRET env var. Confirm the Netlify cron trigger includes this header.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: Confirm that the Netlify scheduled function trigger mechanism supports custom headers before implementing.

Missing RLS Policies on API Credentials and Settings Tables
findings.description: Several core tables do not have Row Level Security (RLS) policies defined for them. Notably: api_credentials, app_settings, provider_settings, model_settings, project_briefing_state. This means any authenticated user might be able to read or modify global settings or other users' credentials if the tables have RLS disabled, or no one can access them if RLS is enabled without policies.

proof_hooks.summary: Comparing created tables vs tables with policies
proof_hooks.command: grep 'CREATE POLICY' supabase/migrations/*.sql and diffing against 'CREATE TABLE'
proof_hooks.actual: api_credentials, app_settings, provider_settings are missing policies

findings.impact: Potential unauthorized access to global app settings, AI model settings, or API credentials, or broken functionality if RLS is enabled but lacks policies granting access.

suggested_fix.approach: Create RLS policies for all identified tables, restricting read/write access to admins or the owning user as appropriate.

history.notes: Verification shows RLS IS enabled with full SELECT/ALL policies on app_settings, provider_settings, model_settings in supabase/migrations/20251214_admin_settings.sql (lines 114-148). api_credentials covered by 001_initial_schema.sql and phase3-migration.sql. project_briefing_state not found in migrations — check 20251212_briefing_state.sql. Marking fixed_pending_verify; run a manual Supabase linter check to confirm.

findings.title: Unhandled null in parseJobResponse when API returns empty results array
findings.description: When the external job API returns {results: null} instead of {results: []}, the parseJobResponse function throws a TypeError. This crashes the ingestion pipeline silently because the catch block logs but does not re-throw or mark the batch as failed. The null case is documented in the API changelog from v2.3.

TypeError thrown when results is null

Run: npx ts-node scripts/ingest.ts --source=mock

findings.impact: Job ingestion silently fails for any API source that returns null instead of empty array. Database stagnates. Users see stale listings.

findings.title: Ingestion pipeline has no retry logic for transient API failures
findings.description: The fetchJobs function makes a single HTTP request per source. If the request fails due to a timeout or 503, the entire source is skipped until the next scheduled run. Given that some APIs have known intermittent availability, a retry with exponential backoff would significantly improve data freshness.

proof_hooks.summary: Single try/catch with no retry loop in fetchJobs
Simulating transient failure shows source is permanently skipped
proof_hooks.expected: Retry and eventually succeed or fail gracefully with clear error
proof_hooks.actual: Source silently skipped, no retry attempted

proof_hooks.summary: Locate actual ingestion pipeline file before attempting fix
steps: Search src/pipeline/, netlify/functions/, src/lib/ for job fetch/ingest logic
If found: verify null guard behavior, update finding with real file reference
If not found: close as 'not applicable' for this codebase version

findings.impact: Missed job data on transient failures. Users may not see jobs from sources that had a momentary outage during the fetch window.

suggested_fix.approach: Wrap fetchJobs call in a retry utility with max 3 attempts, exponential backoff starting at 1s, and a circuit breaker that skips after 3 consecutive full failures for a source.
suggested_fix.risk_notes: Retries increase total API call count. If you are on a metered API plan, add a daily call budget. Also ensure retries do not cause duplicate ingestion by checking idempotency keys.
tests_needed: Unit test: retry utility retries on 503 and succeeds on second attempt
Unit test: retry utility gives up after max attempts and throws
Unit test: retry utility does not retry on 400 (client error)
